panel.origin: The defining instrument of rock and pop — a solid-body guitar whose strings are turned into sound electronically.
panel.chemistry: Endlessly versatile, from clean and shimmering to screaming distortion, shaped entirely by amps and effects pedals.
panel.mixology: Strings are plucked or strummed; magnetic pickups convert their vibration into a signal sent to an amplifier.
panel.examples: Every rock riff and solo; the wail of Hendrix, the crunch of any guitar band.

panel.origin: The hollow-bodied guitar that needs no amplifier — the workhorse of folk, pop, and singer-songwriters.
panel.chemistry: Warm, woody, and resonant, equally suited to gentle fingerpicking and bright rhythmic strumming.
panel.mixology: Strings (steel or nylon) are strummed or picked; the hollow body amplifies the sound acoustically.
panel.examples: The campfire singalong; the intimate backing of any singer-songwriter.

panel.origin: The low-end backbone of nearly every band — it locks together the rhythm and the harmony.
panel.chemistry: Deep, round, and groovy, felt as much as heard, sitting between the drums and the rest of the band.
panel.mixology: Usually four thick strings plucked with fingers or a pick; like an electric guitar but lower and longer.
panel.examples: The groove under any funk, rock, or pop song; the walking line in jazz.

panel.origin: A small, four-stringed Hawaiian cousin of the guitar with a bright, cheerful voice.
panel.chemistry: Light, sweet, and jangly, instantly evoking a sunny, breezy, feel-good mood.
panel.mixology: Four nylon strings strummed or picked; tiny and easy to learn, which fueled its huge popularity.
panel.examples: Indie-pop singalongs; anything aiming for a warm, carefree, tropical feel.

panel.origin: A horizontal steel guitar played on a stand with foot pedals and knee levers — the crying voice of country music.
panel.chemistry: Smooth, swooping, and vocal, sliding fluidly between notes with a weeping, shimmering quality.
panel.mixology: The player slides a steel bar along the strings while pedals and levers bend the pitch of individual strings.
panel.examples: The signature weeping sound of classic country; lush pedal-steel textures in Americana and pop.

panel.origin: An electronic keyboard that generates and shapes sound from scratch — the foundation of electronic music.
panel.chemistry: Almost limitless: warm pads, sharp leads, deep basses, and sounds that resemble no acoustic instrument at all.
panel.mixology: Oscillators create raw waveforms that are shaped by filters and envelopes; controlled by a keyboard or knobs.
panel.examples: The pulsing basslines and soaring leads of synth-pop, EDM, and film scores.

panel.origin: An electromechanical keyboard with a warm, bell-like tone — the soulful sound of 1970s funk and soul.
panel.chemistry: Mellow, warm, and slightly bell-like, with a characteristic 'bark' when played hard.
panel.mixology: Keys strike metal tines or reeds whose vibration is picked up electrically, like a cross between a piano and a guitar.
panel.examples: The lush keys of soul, funk, and jazz fusion; the iconic Rhodes and Wurlitzer sounds.

panel.origin: An electric organ with a swirling, soulful sound, made famous in gospel, jazz, and rock.
panel.chemistry: Rich, warm, and swirling, especially through a rotating Leslie speaker that gives it its signature throb.
panel.mixology: Drawbars blend electronic tones; a foot pedalboard plays bass while both hands work two keyboards.
panel.examples: The soulful swell of gospel and blues; the churning organ of classic rock.

panel.origin: A portable, bellows-driven keyboard with reeds — the heart of folk music across many cultures.
panel.chemistry: Reedy, rich, and full, capable of both melody and accompaniment at once, with a distinctive breathing quality.
panel.mixology: The player squeezes the bellows to push air over reeds while pressing keys or buttons on each side.
panel.examples: French café music, polka, tango, Cajun, and folk traditions across the world.

panel.origin: A small handheld keyboard you blow into — part wind instrument, part piano.
panel.chemistry: Bright, reedy, and breathy, like a cross between a harmonica and a toy organ, with a charming, intimate tone.
panel.mixology: The player blows through a mouthpiece while pressing piano-style keys that open reeds.
panel.examples: Reggae and dub textures; lo-fi and indie tracks reaching for a warm, casual sound.

panel.origin: The assembled set of drums and cymbals played by one person — the engine of rock, pop, and jazz.
panel.chemistry: Powerful and driving, combining the thump of bass drum, snap of snare, and shimmer of cymbals into one groove.
panel.mixology: Played seated, using both hands on drums and cymbals and both feet on the bass drum and hi-hat pedals.
panel.examples: The beat behind virtually every rock, pop, and jazz recording.

panel.origin: A wooden box drum the player sits on and slaps — a portable rhythm section in a single instrument.
panel.chemistry: Punchy and warm, with a deep bass tone in the center and a sharp snare-like snap at the edges.
panel.mixology: The player sits on top and strikes the front face with hands, getting bass and snare sounds from different spots.
panel.examples: Acoustic and unplugged sessions; flamenco, where it became a staple.

panel.origin: Tall, narrow hand drums from Afro-Cuban music, usually played in pairs or threes.
panel.chemistry: Warm, resonant, and rhythmic, ranging from deep open tones to sharp slaps.
panel.mixology: Played standing or seated with the bare hands, using different strokes for distinct pitches and tones.
panel.examples: Latin music, salsa, and Afro-Cuban grooves; percussion layers in funk and pop.

panel.origin: A rope-tuned, goblet-shaped West African hand drum with an enormous range of tones.
panel.chemistry: Hugely dynamic, from a deep resonant bass to a cracking high slap, all from one drumhead.
panel.mixology: Played with bare hands between the knees or on a stand; hand position and strike point change the tone.
panel.examples: West African drumming circles; world-music and fusion percussion.

panel.origin: An electronic device that creates and sequences drum sounds — the heartbeat of hip-hop, techno, and pop.
panel.chemistry: From punchy synthetic kicks and snares to crisp electronic hi-hats; defined the sound of entire genres.
panel.mixology: The user programs a pattern step by step, or plays pads, and the machine loops the beat back.
panel.examples: The iconic beats of hip-hop and electronic music; classic boxes like the Roland 808 and 909.

panel.origin: A small hand frame fitted with jingling metal discs — one of the simplest and most universal rhythm instruments.
panel.chemistry: Bright, jingling, and shimmering, adding sparkle and drive to a backbeat.
panel.mixology: Shaken, tapped against the hand or hip, or struck to mark the rhythm.
panel.examples: The shimmer in gospel and pop choruses; a staple of folk and rock percussion.

panel.origin: A bright, twangy string instrument with a drum-like body — the signature sound of bluegrass and folk.
panel.chemistry: Bright, sharp, and percussive, with a distinctive ringing twang that cuts through any acoustic band.
panel.mixology: Strings are plucked over a round, drumhead-covered body; bluegrass uses a fast three-finger 'rolling' style.
panel.examples: Bluegrass breakdowns; the rolling twang in folk and Americana.

panel.origin: A small, round-backed string instrument with eight strings in four pairs, played with a pick.
panel.chemistry: Bright, crisp, and shimmering, with a fast tremolo that lets it sustain a singing melody.
panel.mixology: Played with a pick; the doubled strings give it a rich, ringing tone, often rapidly tremolo-picked.
panel.examples: Bluegrass and folk; the lyrical mandolin lines in Italian and Celtic music.

panel.origin: The very same instrument as the violin, but played in folk, country, and bluegrass styles.
panel.chemistry: Lively, raw, and rhythmic, leaning into a driving, danceable energy rather than classical polish.
panel.mixology: Held and bowed exactly like a violin, but with folk techniques — drones, slides, and rhythmic 'chops.'
panel.examples: Country and bluegrass hoedowns; Celtic and Appalachian dance tunes.

panel.origin: A tiny handheld reed instrument you play with your breath — pocket-sized, expressive, and soulful.
panel.chemistry: Wailing, breathy, and vocal, from gentle folk melodies to crying, bent blues notes.
panel.mixology: The player breathes in and out through holes; each draws or blows a reed. 'Bending' breath alters the pitch.
panel.examples: The lonesome wail of the blues; folk and country, from Dylan to Little Walter.

panel.origin: A resonator guitar with a metal cone inside that makes it louder and gives it a distinctive metallic ring.
panel.chemistry: Bright, metallic, and resonant, with a sustained, slightly nasal twang, often played with a slide.
panel.mixology: Strings are plucked over a metal resonator cone; often played flat on the lap with a steel bar (slide).
panel.examples: Bluegrass and Americana; the slide-guitar growl of country blues.

panel.origin: A long-necked Indian string instrument with sympathetic strings that give it a shimmering, droning resonance.
panel.chemistry: Rich, buzzing, and hypnotic, with a complex ringing wash from extra strings that vibrate on their own.
panel.mixology: Plucked with a wire pick; the player bends the strings sideways across curved frets to slide between notes.
panel.examples: Indian classical music; the psychedelic shimmer it brought to 1960s rock like the Beatles.

panel.origin: A goblet-shaped West African hand drum, central to traditional drumming and now used worldwide.
panel.chemistry: Dynamic and expressive, with deep bass tones and sharp, cracking slaps from a single skin.
panel.mixology: Played with bare hands; different strokes and hand positions produce its full range of tones.
panel.examples: Traditional West African ensembles; drum circles and world-fusion music everywhere.

panel.origin: A tuned percussion instrument hammered from an oil drum — the national instrument of Trinidad and Tobago.
panel.chemistry: Bright, shimmering, and bell-like, capable of full melodies and harmonies, with a sunny, tropical character.
panel.mixology: The player strikes different hammered sections of the concave drum surface, each tuned to a note, with rubber-tipped sticks.
panel.examples: Caribbean calypso and soca; the unmistakable sound of a steel band at Carnival.

panel.origin: A reed instrument powered by air squeezed from a bag, famous for its continuous droning sound.
panel.chemistry: Loud, piercing, and stirring, with a constant background drone under the melody and no gaps between notes.
panel.mixology: The player blows into a bag (or pumps bellows) and squeezes it to push air through the melody pipe and drones.
panel.examples: Scottish and Irish traditional music; the haunting solo pipes of military and ceremonial occasions.

panel.origin: A long wooden tube from Aboriginal Australia, one of the world's oldest wind instruments.
panel.chemistry: Deep, droning, and resonant, with a hypnotic buzzing pulse and rich overtones.
panel.mixology: The player buzzes their lips into one end and uses 'circular breathing' to sustain the drone without pausing.
panel.examples: Aboriginal Australian ceremony and music; ambient and world-fusion textures.

panel.origin: A thumb piano — metal tines mounted on a wooden board, plucked with the thumbs.
panel.chemistry: Sweet, gentle, and music-box-like, with a soft, soothing, bell-like tone.
panel.mixology: The player holds it in both hands and plucks the metal tines with their thumbs; tine length sets the pitch.
panel.examples: African mbira traditions; calming, lo-fi, and ambient music.

panel.origin: A two-stringed Chinese bowed instrument with a remarkably expressive, voice-like tone.
panel.chemistry: Soulful, singing, and melancholy, capable of expressive slides and vibrato close to the human voice.
panel.mixology: Played upright on the lap; the bow hair is fixed between the two strings, and there's no fingerboard to press against.
panel.examples: Chinese classical and folk music; evocative film scores reaching for an East Asian sound.

panel.origin: Record players used as instruments — the foundation of hip-hop DJing and turntablism.
panel.chemistry: Percussive and rhythmic, turning vinyl into scratches, stabs, and beats manipulated by hand.
panel.mixology: The DJ moves the record by hand against the needle to 'scratch,' and blends two turntables through a mixer.
panel.examples: The scratching and cutting at the birth of hip-hop; the art of turntablism.

panel.origin: A device or software that records snippets of sound and lets you replay them as an instrument.
panel.chemistry: Anything at all — a chopped soul record, a vocal, a single drum hit — replayed, pitched, and looped.
panel.mixology: Sounds are recorded ('sampled'), assigned to pads or keys, and triggered and manipulated in performance.
panel.examples: The chopped-up loops at the heart of hip-hop and electronic music.

panel.origin: Digital Audio Workstation — the software that has become the modern studio and instrument in one.
panel.chemistry: Everything: it records, sequences, synthesizes, and mixes, often replacing a whole room of equipment.
panel.mixology: Producers arrange MIDI and audio on a screen timeline, layering virtual instruments and effects.
panel.examples: How nearly all modern music is made; tools like Ableton Live, Logic, and FL Studio.

panel.origin: A keyboard or pad device that sends control signals (not sound) to software instruments.
panel.chemistry: Itself silent — it triggers whatever virtual instrument it's connected to, from pianos to synths to drums.
panel.mixology: The player presses keys or pads; MIDI data tells the computer which notes and how hard to play them.
panel.examples: The hands-on control surface behind most modern electronic production.

panel.origin: A device that fuses a voice with a synthesizer, producing the classic 'robot voice' sound.
panel.chemistry: Robotic, harmonic, and otherworldly, turning sung or spoken words into chords of synthetic tone.
panel.mixology: The player sings into a mic while playing chords; the voice's character is imposed onto the synth sound.
panel.examples: The robotic vocals of electro and funk; acts like Daft Punk and Kraftwerk.

panel.origin: The original and most universal instrument — the human voice, carried by every culture on Earth.
panel.chemistry: Infinitely expressive, the model every other instrument is measured against, capable of pitch, rhythm, and pure emotion.
panel.mixology: Produced by air from the lungs vibrating the vocal cords, shaped by the throat, mouth, and lips into pitch and words.
panel.examples: Every song ever sung; from opera to rock to a cappella harmony.

panel.origin: Vocal percussion — imitating drum machines and instruments using only the mouth, voice, and breath.
panel.chemistry: Punchy and percussive, mimicking kicks, snares, hi-hats, scratches, and even basslines with startling realism.
panel.mixology: The performer uses lips, tongue, and breath to imitate drum and electronic sounds, often into a microphone.
panel.examples: Hip-hop and a cappella; standalone beatbox performance and battles.

panel.origin: Making rhythm with the body itself — clapping, stomping, snapping, and slapping.
panel.chemistry: Earthy and immediate, an entire groove built from the sounds the body can make with no instrument at all.
panel.mixology: Performers clap hands, stamp feet, slap thighs and chest, and snap fingers in coordinated patterns.
panel.examples: Stomp-style shows; the clap-and-stomp groove of songs like 'We Will Rock You.'

GUITARS & BASS.en: The electric guitar barely makes a sound on its own — almost everything you hear, from clean shimmer to screaming distortion, is created after the strings, in the amp and effects pedals.
GUITARS & BASS.es: The electric guitar barely makes a sound on its own — almost everything you hear, from clean shimmer to screaming distortion, is created after the strings, in the amp and effects pedals.
GUITARS & BASS.fr: The electric guitar barely makes a sound on its own — almost everything you hear, from clean shimmer to screaming distortion, is created after the strings, in the amp and effects pedals.
GUITARS & BASS.it: The electric guitar barely makes a sound on its own — almost everything you hear, from clean shimmer to screaming distortion, is created after the strings, in the amp and effects pedals.

KEYS & SYNTHS.en: The first synthesizers filled entire rooms and were played by patching cables between modules — today a single laptop holds thousands of synth sounds those pioneers could only dream of.
KEYS & SYNTHS.es: The first synthesizers filled entire rooms and were played by patching cables between modules — today a single laptop holds thousands of synth sounds those pioneers could only dream of.
KEYS & SYNTHS.fr: The first synthesizers filled entire rooms and were played by patching cables between modules — today a single laptop holds thousands of synth sounds those pioneers could only dream of.
KEYS & SYNTHS.it: The first synthesizers filled entire rooms and were played by patching cables between modules — today a single laptop holds thousands of synth sounds those pioneers could only dream of.

DRUMS & RHYTHM.en: The Roland TR-808 drum machine flopped commercially and was discontinued in 1983 — then hip-hop and electronic producers adopted its 'unrealistic' sounds, making it one of the most influential instruments ever.
DRUMS & RHYTHM.es: The Roland TR-808 drum machine flopped commercially and was discontinued in 1983 — then hip-hop and electronic producers adopted its 'unrealistic' sounds, making it one of the most influential instruments ever.
DRUMS & RHYTHM.fr: The Roland TR-808 drum machine flopped commercially and was discontinued in 1983 — then hip-hop and electronic producers adopted its 'unrealistic' sounds, making it one of the most influential instruments ever.
DRUMS & RHYTHM.it: The Roland TR-808 drum machine flopped commercially and was discontinued in 1983 — then hip-hop and electronic producers adopted its 'unrealistic' sounds, making it one of the most influential instruments ever.

FOLK & ACOUSTIC.en: A fiddle and a violin are physically the exact same instrument — the only difference is the music played on it and sometimes a slightly flatter bridge to make fast string-crossing easier.
FOLK & ACOUSTIC.es: A fiddle and a violin are physically the exact same instrument — the only difference is the music played on it and sometimes a slightly flatter bridge to make fast string-crossing easier.
FOLK & ACOUSTIC.fr: A fiddle and a violin are physically the exact same instrument — the only difference is the music played on it and sometimes a slightly flatter bridge to make fast string-crossing easier.
FOLK & ACOUSTIC.it: A fiddle and a violin are physically the exact same instrument — the only difference is the music played on it and sometimes a slightly flatter bridge to make fast string-crossing easier.

WORLD INSTRUMENTS.en: The steel pan is one of the only acoustic instruments invented in the 20th century — born in Trinidad from discarded oil drums, hammered into tuned playing surfaces.
WORLD INSTRUMENTS.es: The steel pan is one of the only acoustic instruments invented in the 20th century — born in Trinidad from discarded oil drums, hammered into tuned playing surfaces.
WORLD INSTRUMENTS.fr: The steel pan is one of the only acoustic instruments invented in the 20th century — born in Trinidad from discarded oil drums, hammered into tuned playing surfaces.
WORLD INSTRUMENTS.it: The steel pan is one of the only acoustic instruments invented in the 20th century — born in Trinidad from discarded oil drums, hammered into tuned playing surfaces.

ELECTRONIC & DJ.en: Hip-hop turntablism turned a playback device into an instrument — DJs discovered that moving a record by hand against the needle produced the 'scratch,' a sound no one designed the turntable to make.
ELECTRONIC & DJ.es: Hip-hop turntablism turned a playback device into an instrument — DJs discovered that moving a record by hand against the needle produced the 'scratch,' a sound no one designed the turntable to make.
ELECTRONIC & DJ.fr: Hip-hop turntablism turned a playback device into an instrument — DJs discovered that moving a record by hand against the needle produced the 'scratch,' a sound no one designed the turntable to make.
ELECTRONIC & DJ.it: Hip-hop turntablism turned a playback device into an instrument — DJs discovered that moving a record by hand against the needle produced the 'scratch,' a sound no one designed the turntable to make.

VOICE & HANDS.en: The human voice is the only instrument every culture on Earth shares and the one all others imitate — and a skilled beatboxer can reproduce a full drum machine using nothing but breath and mouth.
VOICE & HANDS.es: The human voice is the only instrument every culture on Earth shares and the one all others imitate — and a skilled beatboxer can reproduce a full drum machine using nothing but breath and mouth.
VOICE & HANDS.fr: The human voice is the only instrument every culture on Earth shares and the one all others imitate — and a skilled beatboxer can reproduce a full drum machine using nothing but breath and mouth.
VOICE & HANDS.it: The human voice is the only instrument every culture on Earth shares and the one all others imitate — and a skilled beatboxer can reproduce a full drum machine using nothing but breath and mouth.
